name: Millbrook Village

notes: Roughly one in five households includes a former Harrow Mill worker or a mill widow
Highest share of fixed-income households in Ashford County
Housing stock: brick storefront apartments and 1880s mill row houses; median year built 1921

character: The old center: brick storefronts on Main Street, mill-era row houses on Chestnut Row, the ruins and the dam and the smokestack that held through forty winters. Older, poorer, prouder than the Crossing across the river. The greenway would be built here — and the tax bill lands here too, on porches where the mailbox still says a dead man's name.

landmarks.name: Stillwater River
landmarks.description: The river that turned the Harrow Mill's wheels for 121 years; slow, brown-green, and patient

landmarks.name: Main Street Bridge
landmarks.description: 1936 concrete arch over the Stillwater, newly posted at 8 tons; the water gauge is bolted to the downstream rail

landmarks.name: Millbrook Farmers Market
landmarks.description: Saturday market on the old mill green — cider stand, jam tables, and the whole town's opinions

landmarks.name: Harrow Mill Ruins
landmarks.description: Brick shell of the Harrow Woolen Mill, closed October 1989; the smokestack still stands over the dam

landmarks.name: The Wheelhouse Diner
landmarks.description: Corner booths, cinnamon rolls, and the unofficial second town hall since 1957

landmarks.name: Millbrook Town Hall
landmarks.description: White clapboard, 1874; the records vault below holds every deed and warrant article in town history

landmarks.name: Main Street
landmarks.description: The village spine: brick storefronts, angled parking, and sidewalks heaved by maple roots

landmarks.name: Corbin's Hardware
landmarks.description: Anchor of the Main Street shops row — hardware and paint downstairs, two rented apartments up

landmarks.name: Millbrook Free Library
landmarks.description: Carnegie-style brick library; home of the mill archive and the large-print mysteries

landmarks.name: Chestnut Row
landmarks.description: 1880s mill workers' row houses, front porches six feet apart; half owned outright, half rented
